ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese fried squid cakes; closest defensible anchor is the general squid food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese chả mực; A0CKP (squid and cuttlefish) used as the closest defensible anchor for a fried squid-based product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese fried squid cake preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chả Mực Chiên Giòn is one plate of approximately 4–6 pieces, weighing around 200 g, commonly served as an appetizer or side dish alongside rice or dipping sauces.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, cooked, fried (FDC ID 175168) combined with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for seasoning and coating adjustments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC entry for fried squid (FDC ID 175168) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat adjusted upward to reflect Vietnamese preparation with fish sauce, fish paste, and deep-frying in vegetable oil, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data. Input record values (protein 17.5 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g, energy 275 kcal, sodium 450 mg) are broadly consistent with this adapted profile and have been retained with minor rounding. Calcium (25 mg), iron (1.0 mg), cholesterol (150 mg), and dietary fibre (0.5 g) are also consistent with USDA fried squid data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered/coated fried seafood and starch-coated protein foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese chả mực chiên giòn. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous starch-coated fried seafood products from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). The dominant carbohydrate source is tapioca/wheat starch coating (6 g/100 g), which has a moderate-to-low GI when combined with high protein (17.5 g) and fat (20 g) that substantially slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 12 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.4 g (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 200 g serving delivers 900 mg sodium, approximately 39–45% of the WHO daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, request reduced fish sauce in preparation, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as fish sauce or soy sauce to keep total sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 6 g/100 g with an estimated GI of 45 (low), but total fat at 20 g/100 g from deep-frying may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–120 g) occasionally, as the low carbohydrate content and high protein limit postprandial glucose rise, but should avoid sweet dipping sauces and monitor total fat intake to prevent chronic insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20 g/100 g), saturated fat (4 g/100 g), cholesterol (150 mg/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and sodium (450 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food only, limit portions to 80–100 g, avoid repeated consumption due to cumulative saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load, and prefer steamed or grilled squid preparations as a lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 450 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold of 400 mg/100 g, contributing to tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations and request steamed or grilled squid patties instead, while reducing fish sauce to lower sodium below 400 mg/100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chả Mực Chiên Giòn (Vietnamese: Chả Mực Chiên Giòn; romanized: Cha Muc Chien Gion) is a popular Vietnamese appetizer and side dish consisting of minced squid mixed with fish paste, seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, shallots, and black pepper, shaped into small patties or cakes, coated lightly in tapioca or wheat starch, and deep-fried until golden brown and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and golden while the interior remains tender and springy, with a pronounced savory umami flavor from the squid and fish sauce. The dish is commonly served with sweet chili sauce or a Vietnamese dipping sauce (nước chấm). Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 275 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 20 g fat (4 g saturated, 0.25 g trans), 6 g carbohydrate, 1 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, 450 mg sodium, 25 mg calcium, 1 mg iron, and 150 mg cholesterol. The high sodium content (450 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without modification. The high fat content from deep-frying raises cardiovascular concern. The low carbohydrate content and estimated glycemic index of 45 (low) make it relatively manageable for blood glucose control in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is a good source of lean seafood protein and provides iron and calcium from squid. It is widely available at Vietnamese restaurants and food delivery platforms across Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: What is Chả Mực Chiên Giòn?
How many calories in crispy fried squid cakes?
Can diabetics eat cha muc chien gion?

Is crispy squid cake suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried squid data (FDC ID 175168) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data. No exact approved-source entry for Vietnamese chả mực chiên giòn exists; values are consistent with the input record and the adapted USDA profile. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact match in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Vietnamese-style deep-fried squid cakes are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as a composite dish as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible): squid contains no meaningful vitamin C and deep-frying destroys any residual heat-labile vitamin C; confirmed negligible per USDA FDC fried squid entry. No core macro (protein_g 17.5, fat_g 20.0, carbohydrate_g 6.0) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on starch-coated fried seafood from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chả mực chiên giòn exists in approved sources. Input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which appears to be a data-entry error; Vietnam is the correct country of origin for this dish and has been used. The input source (FAO/INFOODS) was listed as supporting only 'menu_listing' and has been supplemented with USDA FDC as the primary nutrient anchor.
